title: Delight-to-Utility Ratio

content.statement: Delight enhances value, but cannot replace utility.
content.question: Is your business more delightful than useful, or more useful than delightful? Which does the customer actually buy?
content.equation: DUR = Delight Score ÷ Core Utility Score
sop: List your product or service's core utility, the one problem it solves regardless of any nice touches.
List every delight feature separately: packaging, tone, extras, design flourishes.
Rate Delight Score and Core Utility Score each 1-10 based on customer feedback, not your own taste (see calculation, page 5).
If utility scores low, fix that before adding any more delight.
If utility is strong, add one delight touch and measure whether it changes repeat purchase, not just first impressions.
content.explainer: Delight without utility is a gimmick. Utility without delight is a commodity. The ratio matters.
questions: Is your business more delightful than useful, or more useful than delightful? Which does the customer actually buy?
If you stripped every delightful touch from your product this week, would people still pay for what's left?
What's the one 'nice' feature you keep polishing that's never once been the reason someone bought from you?
content.equation_example: Rate Delight Score and Core Utility Score each 1-10. Delight 8 divided by Utility 4 equals 2.0. A ratio above 1 means you're leaning decorative, delight is outpacing the actual problem you solve. A ratio at or below 1 means utility is carrying the product, which is the safer place to be until utility is maxed out.
content.closing_statement: Solve the problem first; charm is the bonus.

caption:
Principle Nº 90: Delight-to-Utility Ratio

Delight enhances value, but cannot replace utility.

Ask yourself: Is your business more delightful than useful, or more useful than delightful? Which does the customer actually buy?
